cards.back: The maximum number of gate inputs a single output can reliably drive while maintaining valid logic levels. Limited by the saturation condition IB > IC/hFE.

cards.front: What is the load current IL per gate in the DTL analysis?

cards.back: IB > (IC + N × IL) / hFE. With the lecture values: 0.4 > (2.182 + 0.82N) / 30, giving N ≤ 11 (practically ≈ 10).

cards.front: What are the valid logic level voltage ranges?
cards.back: Logic LOW: < 0.4V. Logic HIGH: > 2.4V. The gap between 0.4V and 2.4V is the forbidden zone.

cards.front: What is P(1) — power consumption when output is HIGH?
cards.back: P(1) = Vcc × IS = 5V × 0.82 mA = 4.1 mW. Only the pull-up resistor current flows (T is OFF).

cards.front: What is P(0) — power consumption when output is LOW?
cards.back: P(0) = Vcc × (I1 + I2) = 5V × (0.56 + 2.182) mA = 5 × 2.742 = 13.71 mW. The saturated transistor draws significant collector current.

cards.front: What is the average power consumption of the DTL gate?
Why is P(0) much larger than P(1)?
cards.back: When output is LOW, T is saturated and IC = 2.182 mA flows through RC. When output is HIGH, T is OFF and only IL = 0.82 mA flows through the pull-up. The collector current dominates.

cards.front: Why does CMOS have much lower static power than DTL?
cards.back: In CMOS, PMOS and NMOS are never both ON simultaneously. One always blocks the current path from Vcc to ground, making static power ≈ 0.

cards.front: How many gates would consume approximately 1W at 9mW each?
cards.back: About 111 gates (1000 mW / 9 mW ≈ 111). For comparison, a modern CPU has billions of CMOS gates and consumes only tens of watts — proving why CMOS dominates.
